speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us that the-- the Sandersons introduced you as a Jewish girl. Did you experience that with other people in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't meet many other people. The only people I really met were other refugee girls who were working in households all over England. And we-- we were-- we felt like sisters, because we had so much in common. But outside of that, I met very few English people, very few. The only people I also met was when I went to Slough. After we met-- moved-- after I moved to Slough, there were other refugee families and we were together with them. No other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying you arrived to the United States and went to Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your relatives wait for you at the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, we-- we were in-- in New York. And I had the-- my-- my father's youngest sister and her husband were in New York. And they picked us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first reaction to the American relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were not-- they were also from Germany. But we were very tired. It was a-- a very emotional coming into the-- I don't think it was Ellis Island, but it was something like Ellis Island. We were examined and we-- we didn't know for sure whether they felt we were in health, good health. We were told that there were some people that weren't left-- let in. They examined. They had something special with the eyes and the chest. And if everything wasn't 100%, they sent you back. You couldn't get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all this was-- we had nothing to eat almost until afternoon. We were hungry. None of us slept very much the night before. It was all very exciting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went to my aunt. And we were very anxious to get to a place where we knew we were going to stay. So I remember my parents sending a telegram to my aunt and saying, you must come, use any excuse, just so we weren't transitory anymore. We wanted to be somewhere where we could put our roots down and start a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the relatives in Chicago, were they Americans? Were they here before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was only my aunt who-- they were all refugees. And my uncle, her husband, died just before they left, she and her two daughters left Germany. He had a heart attack. He never wanted to leave-- he was in the First World War-- and I guess that became very evident, because he just dropped dead. And-- and so she was alone with the two girls. She was happy to see us. She needed that support.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left-- they came a year and a half before us. All that before the Kristallnacht, they didn't go through all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were these cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- Doris was-- is-- is one year younger than I. And Margaret is almost six years younger than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that they were already acclimated to the States, and that they treated you differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because when we arrived here and I saw Doris, I wrote in my diary she looked like a movie star, so completely different. And-- and we-- I just happened to read this the-- the-- last night. I was-- I didn't know it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you relate to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. We were very good-- we're good friends and-- and-- always very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister part of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Oh, yes. The four of us, when we were little, we always had to wear the same clothes, and I hated that. I wasn't very happy with that, the same dresses, the same skirts and blouses. And it wasn't my-- my thing at all. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first meet Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the factory. The day after we arrived here, my father and I went downtown from factory to factory to try and find work. And I found work the first day. My father didn't. And I worked in a dress factory. And I met-- and all the girls there were not-- not Jewish. They were all Americans. Most of them were Italian. And I became very close friends with one of the Italian girls. And that's when I met Americans. And I couldn't believe it. They all looked like movie stars to me too. They wear makeup. And when they were finished with work they put on these beautiful clothes and-- and they were so chic and so-- so up-to-date with everything. And I admired that. It was very interesting. But I didn't stay in the factory too long. And I'll tell you why. One Saturday, there was a bar mitzvah somewhere in our-- in our friend circle. And I told the-- and I wanted to get off work. I had to work six days a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told the-- the head of the department, I asked her, is it-- I cannot come in next-- on Saturday. Is that all right? And she said to me, do you want to quit? And I had no idea what that meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I thought it meant you don't want to come in or whatever, and-- or maybe I won't get paid for the day. And I said yes. So when I came in Monday morning, there was somebody standing at my table, somebody else. And I was-- that was it. I couldn't go back. I told them I didn't know what quit-- it didn't mean anything. And so I had to look for another job. And I found a job with a shoe factory. And-- and the factory-- the people, the owners were also former Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the dress manufacturer, the-- the-- what was the company's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not right now. I should, but I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Italian girls that worked with you treated you differently? Did they ask you questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they all wanted to know whether we-- whether we have-- whether in Germany we lived in regular houses, like stone houses, and whether we ate from dishes, or-- they-- they had no idea. So I showed them some pictures of the-- of the houses that we lived in. They couldn't believe it. None of them lived in such a nice house.

speaker: interviewer
text: The shoe factory you went to, do you remember the name of the shoe factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right. I thought you asked me, what was it that you asked me before?

speaker: interviewer
text: The dress factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That was something like-- like Fancy-- Fancy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Frocks. I think that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the shoe factory? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know the family who owned it was the family Strauss. That I remember. Was it the-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Oh. Sorry. Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember. I think I-- I knew it yesterday, but I don't remember it today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Jewish-- were they Jewish owners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. Former-- also refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the workers were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost all of them. Many of them. The foreman was Jewish, and many of the people I worked with there. They gave us jobs that they-- they paid very, very low price, paid us very, very poorly.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you-- every young person you looked at looked like a movie star. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How did that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to-- I tried very hard to be like them. I tried to get as much information as I could. And I think that also stimulated me to choose the profession that I never dreamed of before, because I always wanted to study medicine. And the second best, I thought, would be as a beautician and I could do massaging and this. Now I have to tell you that before I made that decision, somebody told me that-- at the-- at the HIAS in Chicago they would test young men and women for their ability to do more than work in a factory. And they would supply them with all the money needed for a further education. And I was-- I was thrilled. I went there. And I was tested. I didn't tell my parents. I did it. I went there. I was tested. By that time, I was 20 years old and-- and took a whole day, all kinds of questions and-- and-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to put things together. I mean, it was a real-- a real-- all-- all-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I'm not-- aptitude tests, in every way, both psychological, mental, physical. And I passed it very high, I'm sure, because they came out and they said, they-- the man-- the man who was conducting this, you can choose any profession you want. We will stand behind you. We will-- we will support you. And I was in heaven. And I went home and I told my parents. And my father said, we don't accept charity. And I said, that isn't charity. They-- they told me that once I-- I am able to support myself, I could slowly pay it back. My father wouldn't allow it. Now, in those days, I didn't know that at age 20 that you can-- that you can make your own decisions. But I also knew that my parents needed that financial support. I was making $10 a week, 6 days a week, working from 8:00 in the morning until 6 o'clock at night. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they needed my support. And so I didn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister doing at the same time.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did she then? She was still going to school. She went to [INAUDIBLE] High School and graduated. And then she worked for the American Jewish Congress.

speaker: interviewer
text: She continued her education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She went-- also went to college in between. I mean, they-- I think an evening class. And I took evening classes too. But when I started with the beauty culture, I had a lot to study. It was a lot-- and I didn't speak English so perfectly, and all the names of the muscles and the-- the blood circulation, and was all-- and everything up to the chest, including the chest. And I had to study very hard. And when I tried to get to-- and I was working on top of it-- to night school to learn public speaking I took up, I fell asleep. I couldn't-- I did it for one or two semesters, but I couldn't keep it up. And she did. And she--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you finished beauty school--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked at the [INAUDIBLE] beauty salon. And then the owner of the store was inducted into the Army. And I took over the management of the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I got married, until I-- until-- no. Even longer, until my child was born, until Diane was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in the Jewish community center at a dance that was set up by the-- by the center in Chicago for soldiers and sailors. And the ladies cooked dinner for them for Saturday night. And then we-- we were very, very-- we were interviewed-- and hours of interview before they accepted us. And then we got our little sign here that said who we were and where-- I think where we came from and a picture. And that's when we were accepted. And we were then classified as-- I don't remember what they-- how they did it, but we could come any Saturday night. And I went there a couple of times and-- and then next Saturday night I came. And all of a sudden, I saw all the-- all the boys on one side. The times before, we were all mixed. All the boys on one side, all the girls on the other side. And then I heard girls yelling, Eddie, you didn't dance with me all night. And then I realized there was one fellow with the girls there. And he danced one after the other, danced around, and kept looking at me over the girl's shoulder. And I said, this guys is a wild one, anything to do with him. And then he came and asked me to dance, and I said, no, thank you. And he said, that's OK. I don't want to dance anymore either. And he sat down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he said in his broken German, [GERMAN], do you want to be my girl? And I thought, this guy is unbelievable. Who ever heard of such a thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how we met. [LAUGHS] Then I invited him for Friday night, him and another fellow. And they came for dinner. And he did-- made himself very unpopular, because he-- my-- my-- we were used-- we were not used to American fellows.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the dinner-- we made a wonderful dinner. And after dinner, she said, do you want your dessert now or later? And everybody said, we're so full. Let's-- we'll wait 'til later. And about an hour or so, we just talked. And he said, Mrs. Meyer, I think I would like to have my dessert now. Who would ever say such a thing? You wait for the hostess to offer it. So we all looked at each other. And I said to myself, he'll never be asked to come here again. And-- but he was. He brought my mother roses, the first time. Not me, brought it for my mother. So-- How long have you been dating before you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- this was in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] November. And the following June we became engaged, and we married in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three. When he left for overseas, we were just two. And when he came back, we were three. When he left, we didn't-- I didn't even know I was pregnant. And she was born [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 8 months and 27 days after we got married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband was gone 26 months overseas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Philippines.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you got married did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only days. Actually, it was only supposed to be three days. And then I contracted front-- trench mouth. And they were afraid to send him. And that's how after our marriage in California-- I went to California to get married, right here, Riverside. And we were together for six weeks after that, because of this trench mouth. And it was very infectuous, very contagious. So it was only supposed to be from Friday 'til Monday. And we were lucky that we were together a little longer and he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was here. He was to be shipped out. We were supposed to get married in-- in September, in Chicago. But he was-- from one day to the next, he was shipped out. He was-- he left. He was in-- in Florida then. And he called me and told me in German, broken German, that he was being shipped and I should come to get-- California. My parents said, no way. You're not going to go away like some floozy or something. It's out of the question. And my husband then decided to call like at 4 o'clock in the morning every night for a week, until my parents said, go already. We got to have our night's sleep. And I went and we were married just-- just to-- Rabbi [INAUDIBLE] with a tallit, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] 1942-- 1943. Diane was born 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I came back to Chicago, had my baby. And we were together for 18 months in my parents' apartment, until my husband came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you wind up in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean after-- we-- we fell in love with California when we were stationed here. And we said, some day we're going to come back. We absolutely have to. It was wonderful. November we were swimming in the ocean. And we did. And then we finally decided, after five years, 1951, my husband packed up the car and-- filled with books. That's the only thing he brought, because we have a lot of books. And he came here and found work and we came afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your husband's name and what-- what is his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- his name is Edward William, it used to be Elkofsky. It was changed. We changed it to Elcott after Diane was born. And he began-- he went-- he went to college for a number of years. He only had one year of college when we got married. And my-- and I worked. The first year that he went back to college here, I worked and supported the family as a-- a beautician. And he-- and then he became-- he started teaching. And in 1968, he transferred from high school to teaching in the college. He started at the college. He was the-- one of the-- he was one of the very first professionals in the-- in that-- when that college opened up, West Los Angeles College.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you have how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three children. David-- Diane was born in 1944, May 30th. David was born August 11th, 1949. And then came-- we said, this is it. That's fine. We don't want any more children. It was very hard. We worked very hard. Money was difficult to come by. And then one time-- one day we had company. A-- by-- a couple of friends of ours came with a newborn baby. And we put the baby there on this bench in a little crib and the-- the-- our children loved this little baby. And after they left, they said, we'd like to have another baby. At that time, Diane was almost 14, David was almost 9. And I said, no way. They really got after us. And we finally said, you always wanted a swimming pool. We can't afford a swimming pool and a baby. So you have to decide on what you want to do. And two days later, they came in the morning and they said, we decided. We want another baby. That's how Shalom came about. He was born nine months later. And his name was then Steven Carl, Carl after my father. And we called him at his birth Shalom. And when Shalom was nine, I took him to Israel. We went on an [NON-ENGLISH] and learned Hebrew. And we both loved Israel so much. And then Eddie came. But neither one of us could find work, so we came back here. But Shalom always talked about going back to Israel. And so when he was 15, we sent him to Solomon Schechter School in Givat Washington, Kibbutz Yavne. And there he went to school for a year. And when he came back, he said, I don't want to be Steven anymore. I want to be Shalom. That's how it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you belong to a Jewish congregation now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes we belong to what used to be called the Valley Jewish Community Center. We joined it in 1951. And it's now called Temple Adat Ari El. And it's in North Hollywood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: To who?

speaker: interviewer
text: To your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents stayed in Chicago. They came every August for a month to be with us, to stay here. And it was-- that was just wonderful. And then my father became ill, started to have polyps in the bladder. And finally in 1955, he died. And he always used to say, when one of us dies, [? Muti ?] will move to California. And that's exactly what happened. [? Muti ?] came to California. And she was-- by that time, had been worked for some time as a baby nurse. And she continued that here. And she moved to an apartment just one mile from here. And as she grew older, she started to work as a volunteer at the Kaiser Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: You showed me this statue that you have next to you. Could you pick it up and tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This statue-- I told you about the crystal ball. The crystal ball went with us. It was one of the few things that were saved. There were no more beautiful dishes that my mother had, no more Chinese teacups that you could see through. That was all gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the crystal ball was very special. And it first was sent from Germany to England in the lift, and then from England to the United States to Chicago. It came-- everything, every time it was transported, it came out alive and was admired by anyone and loved by-- by everyone, especially by my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- [SIGHS] it was standing on the buffet that was cut into two at that time when we saved the ball. Then, [SIGHS] when my mother died, it was supposed to go to my sister. And she had a very small place at that-- and she said, I think maybe the oldest granddaughter should have it. And that was Diane, my daughter. And Diane and [? Muti ?] were always very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, so Diane was the lucky recipient of the ball. Then 1940-- 1992-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes. Was it '92? The earthquake came in Northridge, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived right above the epicenter. when it hit-- and it was very strong. It was a very, very terrible earthquake. Diane screamed, [? Muti's ?] ball, [? Muti's ?] ball. They ran into the living room. But the-- there was a lot of noise anyway, because everything in their house was broken. The ball was on the floor, totally in pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change tapes.
